candidate_problems.text: Can the conjecture be made into a precise, non-circular criterion for when a record is 'mutually consistent and stably copyable,' and does that criterion uniquely recover the effective pointer basis and classical definiteness across realistic measurement models? The open tension is that 'knowledge-bearing system,' 'record,' and 'copyability' currently do major explanatory work but are not yet sharply defined in a way that clearly exceeds standard decoherence/quantum Darwinism.

Does branch-relative record survival actually explain experienced single-outcome definiteness, or does it merely redescribe Everettian branching in epistemic language without solving the preferred-outcome/preferred-observer problem? The unresolved issue is whether indexical self-location is enough, or whether the conjecture still presupposes the very notion of an observer finding themselves in one stable record-sequence that it aims to explain.

Can Born-weighted self-location be derived from the consistency-and-copyability framework alone, rather than imported from separate decision-theoretic or typicality assumptions? This is a central open question because the conjecture explicitly shifts the hard problem from dynamics to self-locating uncertainty, but unless the weighting rule follows from the proposed explanatory core, a major part of quantum prediction remains externally attached.
